title: Rate-Distortion Bounds on Operator Compression
subtitle: When do embeddings provably fail? Shannon's theory tells us the minimum dimensions needed for each Schankian operator

children.children: Every embedding model makes a bet: that the structure in your data can be compressed into a low-dimensional vector without losing too much information. But how do you know when that bet pays off — and when it provably fails?

Shannon's rate-distortion theory gives an exact answer. It tells you the MINIMUM number of bits (and therefore embedding dimensions) needed to represent a relationship at a given accuracy. If the required rate is high, no embedding trick will save you.

Rate-distortion theory is like a speed-of-light limit for compression. No matter how clever your embedding algorithm, you cannot compress below R(D) bits at distortion level D. This gives us a principled way to ask: 'Is this operator simple enough to embed, or is it fundamentally too complex?'

Imagine you have a source — say, the set of all entities that participate in ATRANS (abstract transfer) events. For each entity, you know its full list of transfer partners. The question is: how many bits do you need to represent each entity so you can still predict its partners accurately?

items.definition: The entity and its full relational profile — who it transfers to/from, under what conditions. This is the 'truth' we want to approximate.
The entity's embedding — a compressed representation. From this alone, we must predict relational structure.
How much we lose. Could be Hamming distance (wrong link predictions), squared error (distance in embedding space), or any task-relevant measure.
Bits per entity needed for the compressed representation. Directly relates to embedding dimensionality: d dimensions of float32 ≈ 32d bits.
The theoretical minimum rate to achieve distortion ≤ D. This is the fundamental limit. Below R(D) bits, distortion D is impossible to achieve — by ANY method.

children.children: R(D) = min_{p(x̂|x): E[d(X,X̂)] ≤ D} I(X; X̂)

In words: among ALL possible encodings that achieve average distortion at most D, find the one that requires the minimum mutual information between the source and its reconstruction. That minimum mutual information IS R(D).
For a Gaussian source with variance σ² and squared-error distortion: R(D) = ½ log(σ²/D) when D < σ². This means halving the allowed distortion costs exactly 0.5 extra bits per symbol.
For discrete sources (like knowledge graphs), the computation is harder but tools like the Blahut-Arimoto algorithm give numerical solutions.
Here's where it gets interesting for our operator project. Different Schankian operators have different relational structures — and therefore different R(D) curves.

props.title: ATRANS vs. PROPEL: Different Compression Profiles

children.children: ATRANS (abstract transfer: giving, selling, lending) has highly structured patterns. Money flows from buyers to sellers. Goods flow the opposite way. There's a strong regularity: entities cluster into 'givers' and 'receivers' with predictable roles.
This means ATRANS has LOW R(D) — a few embedding dimensions capture most of the structure. The entity's 'giver/receiver' profile compresses well.

Now consider PROPEL (physical force application). A ball can be thrown by anyone at anything. The patterns are more idiosyncratic — who throws what where depends on specific situations, not general roles.
PROPEL may have HIGHER R(D) for the same distortion target. More dimensions are needed because the relational structure is less compressible.

If R(D) ≈ log|S| (the maximum possible), the graph has essentially NO exploitable structure for that operator. Entity behavior is so idiosyncratic that each entity needs its own unique representation — embeddings can't generalize. This is the formal condition for 'embeddings provably fail.'

props.title: The Key Quantity: Conditional Mutual Information

children.children: The central measurement that determines embeddability is:
I(Entity; Targets | Operator)

This measures: once you know WHICH operator is being applied, how much does the specific entity matter for predicting outcomes? High values mean entities are idiosyncratic even within an operator type — hard to compress.
In a restaurant script, I(Entity; Targets | ATRANS) might be low for the 'pay' action — almost all customers transfer money to servers. The entity barely matters; the operator determines everything.
But I(Entity; Targets | INGEST) might be higher — what each person orders to eat is idiosyncratic. You need more bits per entity to predict their ingestion targets.

props.title: Connection to Information Bottleneck (#39) and MDL (#45)

children.children: Three brainstorm entries turn out to be facets of the same mathematical object:

• The Information Bottleneck curve (Entry #39) IS the rate-distortion curve — they are equivalent optimization problems. IB compresses relevant information; R-D compresses to a distortion target. Same Lagrangian, different parameterization.
• MDL (Entry #45) provides an UPPER BOUND on R(D). The minimum description length of a model is always ≥ R(D). MDL is achievable; R(D) is the limit MDL approaches.
• β in IB = the slope of the R(D) curve at the operating point. High β → aggressive compression → few operators. Low β → more operators, finer distinctions.
This unification is powerful: it means our operator discovery (via IB/MDL) is provably approaching the best-possible compression (R(D)).
Here's how to actually measure this on real knowledge graphs:

items.definition: Take a KG like Freebase or ConceptNet. Group triples by relation (is-a, part-of, causes, etc.). Each relation type approximates a Schankian operator.
For each relation r, build the bipartite graph of (head entity → tail entity). This is the structure the embedding must capture.
Use the Blahut-Arimoto algorithm to compute the rate-distortion function for each operator's adjacency structure. Use Hamming distortion (binary: did we predict the correct tail?).
Train embeddings (TransE, RotatE, etc.) at various dimensions d. Plot achieved distortion vs. rate (≈ 32d bits). Compare to R(D) curve.
How far are embeddings from R(D)? For simple relations, the gap should be small. For complex, idiosyncratic relations, the gap may be large — or R(D) itself may be too high for practical embedding.

children.children: We predict: hierarchical relations (is-a, part-of) will have LOW R(D) — tree structure compresses beautifully. Causal relations (causes, enables) will have MEDIUM R(D). Idiosyncratic relations (located-in for arbitrary entities) will have HIGH R(D). This would explain why embedding methods work great for type hierarchies but struggle with arbitrary factual relations.

props.title: Why This Matters for Operator Architecture

children.children: Rate-distortion analysis doesn't just tell us IF embeddings work — it tells us HOW to design the operator architecture:

Think of each operator as a wardrobe. R(D) tells you how big the wardrobe needs to be. A TRANSFER operator might need a small wardrobe — a few shelves distinguishing 'giver types' from 'receiver types'. But a PERCEIVE operator might need a walk-in closet — every perceiver has unique sensory patterns.

Rather than giving every operator the same-sized wardrobe (same embedding dimension), R(D) analysis lets us allocate dimensions proportionally. Simple operators get fewer dimensions; complex ones get more. This is provably optimal resource allocation.
Suppose R(D=0.1) for ATRANS is 12 bits and for PROPEL is 48 bits. Then:
• ATRANS rotation matrices could operate in a 4-dimensional subspace (sufficient to encode ~12 bits of structure)
• PROPEL rotation matrices need a 16-dimensional subspace (to encode ~48 bits)

This connects directly to Entry #55's full rotation matrix architecture: different operators could act on different-sized subspaces of the concatenated entity embedding, with sizes determined by R(D) analysis.
There's an elegant way to think about embedding-based prediction as communication through a noisy channel:

The embedding process is an encoder: it maps each entity to a point in ℝᵈ. The scoring function (dot product, distance, rotation) is the channel: it takes two embeddings and produces a prediction. Noise comes from the fact that many different relational profiles map to nearby points.
Channel capacity C = max I(X;Y) over input distributions. For a Gaussian channel: C = ½ log(1 + SNR). An embedding of dimension d with bounded norm has capacity ≈ d · ½ log(1 + SNR) bits.

The fundamental requirement: C ≥ R(D). If the channel capacity of your embedding space is less than the rate-distortion of the operator, no training algorithm can achieve distortion D. You need more dimensions, period.
The most valuable prediction from R-D analysis: identifying relations where NO fixed-dimension embedding can work.
Consider the relation 'born-on(person, date)'. Each person has exactly one birthday, chosen from 365 options. For N people, the adjacency matrix is essentially random — no structural regularity.
R(D≈0) ≈ log(365) ≈ 8.5 bits per person. That's not terrible — about 3 embedding dimensions could handle it.

But now consider 'phone-number(person, number)'. With 10¹⁰ possible numbers, R(D≈0) ≈ 33 bits. You'd need ~10 dedicated dimensions just for phone numbers. And this relation has almost NO transferable structure — knowing Alice's phone number tells you nothing about Bob's.
This is why knowledge graph embeddings work well for relational/structural knowledge but poorly for arbitrary facts. R(D) makes this intuition rigorous.

TRUE semantic primitives should have LOW R(D) — that's what makes them 'primitive.' They capture regularities that compress well. If a proposed operator has R(D) ≈ log|entities|, it's not capturing a genuine pattern — it's just a lookup table in disguise. R(D) analysis is a TEST OF PRIMITIVITY.

questions.question: What does R(D) represent in rate-distortion theory?
options: The maximum distortion achievable at rate R
The minimum bits per symbol needed to achieve distortion ≤ D
The optimal embedding dimension for a given dataset
The compression ratio of the best known algorithm
questions.explanation: R(D) is the minimum mutual information (bits) between source and reconstruction needed to guarantee average distortion at most D. It's a fundamental lower bound — no encoding can do better.

questions.question: If I(Entity; Targets | Operator) is very low for a particular operator, what does that imply?
options: The operator is useless — it doesn't distinguish entities
Entities are highly idiosyncratic under this operator
The operator captures strong regularities — entities are predictable from operator type alone
The embedding needs more dimensions
questions.explanation: Low conditional mutual information means knowing which entity it is doesn't add much information beyond knowing the operator. The operator captures most of the structure — entities behave predictably under it. This is ideal for compression.

questions.question: How does R(D) connect to the Information Bottleneck (Entry #39)?
options: They are unrelated — IB is about relevance, R-D is about fidelity
IB always gives a tighter bound than R(D)
They are equivalent optimization problems with different parameterizations
R(D) is a special case of IB for Gaussian sources
questions.explanation: The IB curve and the R(D) curve are equivalent — both trace the Pareto frontier of compression vs. information preservation. The IB Lagrange multiplier β corresponds to the slope of R(D). This unification means our IB-based operator discovery is provably approaching optimal compression.

questions.question: What does it mean if a proposed Schankian operator has R(D) ≈ log|entities|?
options: It's the most fundamental primitive
It compresses perfectly
It's not capturing a genuine pattern — it's essentially a lookup table
It needs to be combined with another operator
questions.explanation: R(D) ≈ log|entities| means each entity needs essentially its own unique code — there's no shared structure to exploit. The operator isn't identifying a real pattern; entity behavior is completely idiosyncratic. True primitives should have LOW R(D), reflecting compressible regularity.

questions.question: Why might different operators need different embedding dimensions?
options: Aesthetic preference for variety in architecture
Each operator's R(D) dictates the minimum dimensions needed — some relations compress more than others
Larger operators need more parameters by definition
GPU memory constraints require splitting dimensions
questions.explanation: Different relational structures have different compressibility. ATRANS might compress into 4 dimensions while PROPEL needs 16. R(D) analysis tells us exactly how many dimensions each operator requires, enabling optimal allocation rather than one-size-fits-all.

children.children: Rate-distortion analysis opens several exciting research directions for the operator project:

Instead of fixed-dimension embeddings, use R(D) to design adaptive architectures. Compute R(D) per operator on training data. Allocate dimensions proportionally. Operators that capture strong regularities get compact representations; complex operators get more capacity.

This connects to the MoE architecture (Entry #62): different experts could have different hidden dimensions, with sizes guided by R(D) analysis. The router doesn't just select which expert — it selects what capacity is needed.

If Schankian operators are universal, their R(D) profiles should be similar across languages. Compute R(D) for 'giving' events in English, Mandarin, Swahili. If the curves match despite surface-level differences, that's strong evidence for language-independent semantic primitives.
This connects to Entry #47's semantic renormalization: R(D) curves should be RG invariants — the same at every scale of description.

Rate-distortion theory transforms 'do embeddings work?' from an empirical question into a mathematical one. It tells us BEFORE we train: which operators will embed well, how many dimensions each needs, and where to expect fundamental failures. This is the theoretical foundation the embedding community has been missing.
